data.title: Ringgit Opens Near Eight-Year High, Strengthens Across The Board
data.description:
The ringgit opened firmer on Feb 13, hovering near its strongest level in almost eight years, supported by persistent weakness in the US dollar and improving sentiment towards Malaysia’s economic outlook.

At 8am, the local currency rose to 3.8945/9110 against the greenback from its close of 3.8995/9060 on Feb 12, approaching levels last seen in April 2018.

The ringgit also strengthened broadly against major currencies, edging up versus the Japanese yen to 2.5494/5604, appreciating against the euro to 4.6224/6420 and gaining against the British pound to 5.3035/3260.

It also advanced against regional peers, improving against the Singapore dollar to 3.0850/0986, rising versus the Thai baht to 12.5257/5885, strengthening against the Indonesian rupiah to 231.4/232.5, and inching higher against the Philippine peso to 6.70/6.73.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Opens Slightly Higher Ahead Of CNY
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia posted modest gains in early Feb 13 trade, with turnover remaining thin as investors adopted a cautious stance ahead of next week’s Chinese New Year (CNY) break.

At 9.02am, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) edged down 1.28 points to 1,749.57, after opening lower at 1,747.89, from Feb 12’s close of 1,750.85.

Losers outnumbered gainers on the broader market, 251 to 105, while 258 counters were unchanged. Market turnover stood at 169.47 million shares valued at RM99.14 million.

Top gainers included Lysaght Galvanized Steel Bhd increased 13 sen to RM2.38, Ta Ann Holdings Bhd added 11 sen to RM4.63, MISC Bhd up nine sen to RM8.19, AMMB Holdings Bhd grew five sen to RM6.54 and Atlan Holdings Bhd gained four sen to RM2.79, while notable decliners were Nestle (M) Bhd down 50 sen to RM110.10, Malaysin Pacific Industries Bhd declined 22 sen to RM30.78, KLCC decreased 14 sen to RM9.01 and both Hong Leong Bank Bhd and United Plantations Bhd eased 10 sen to RM24.60 and RM29.52 respectively.

Investors remained selective, with light trading activity reflecting caution ahead of the holiday period.

data.title: Bursa Opens Tepidly, FBM KLCI Dips Amid Cautious Trading
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia started the trading day on a softer note, with most major indices retreating marginally in early deals.

As at 9.07am, the benchmark FBM KLCI slipped 1.03 points, or 0.06%, to 1,738.51.

The broader indices also recorded early declines: the FBM70 eased 32.37 points, or 0.18%, to 17,537.30, while the F4GBM lost 0.53 of a point, or 0.05%, to 1,025.69. Meanwhile, the FBMEMAS dropped 10.14 points, or 0.08%, to 12,728.06, and the FBMSHA declined 6.02 points, or 0.05%, to 12,215.99.

Most active counters in early trading were HONGSENG, which gained 0.005 to 0.010 on a volume of 1.26 million shares, ZENTECH [S] at 0.005 with 77,865 shares traded, and TWL [S] at 0.025 on 50,771 shares. 99SMART and ARTRONIQ [S] also saw notable activity, with ARTRONIQ up 0.010 to 0.165.

In terms of price movers, MNRB led the gainers, rising 0.240 to 2.700, followed by CHINTEK (+0.140 to 10.580), F&N [S] (+0.100 to 33.000), PETGAS (+0.080 to 18.300), and GOHUB (+0.055 to 0.945).

On the downside, HEIM fell 0.260 to 24.340, MPI [S] dropped 0.260 to 29.520, 99SMART eased 0.110 to 3.840, KEINHIN [S] declined 0.090 to 1.160, and UTDPLT [S] slipped 0.080 to 30.100.

Market watchers said early caution reflects investors digesting regional and global cues while awaiting fresh catalysts to drive trading momentum.

data.title: Hong Kong Stocks Open Lower As Hang Seng Drifts From Recent Highs
data.description:
Hong Kong equities opened on a cautious note on Feb 13, with the benchmark Hang Seng Index declining at the open as investors weighed mixed global cues and sector pressures. Early trading showed the Hang Seng dipping as market participants remained tentative, particularly ahead of key macro data and continued technology sector scrutiny.

The early weakness followed a modest drop in the index in the previous session, with market forecasts pointing to a softer start amid global headwinds and concern over artificial intelligence‑led volatility in tech stocks.

Sentiment is tempered as traders look for fresh catalysts to sustain broader participation beyond recent swings, with heavyweight tech and financial names in focus for direction later in the session.

data.title: South Korea’s Kospi Slips As Profit‑Taking Ends Four‑Day Rally
data.description:
South Korean equities pulled back on Feb 13, with the benchmark Korea Exchange Kospi closing at 5,507.01, down 15.26 points, or 0.28%, snapping a four‑day winning streak despite briefly touching a fresh intraday high.

The retreat came as investors booked gains ahead of next week’s extended Lunar New Year market holiday, taking profits after recent strength. Foreign selling weighed on the market, offsetting retail and institutional interest.

Trade was robust, with heavy volume as sentiment shifted following overnight losses in US technology stocks, prompting caution in Asia.

Despite the pullback, South Korea’s equity market has shown resilience, with key tech names helping buoy the broader benchmark even as broader weakness emerged late in the session.

Investors now eye catalysts after the holiday break, including macroeconomic data and global tech trends, for clues on the next directional move.

data.title: Bursa Closes Lower As Key Indices Slip, Active Counters Mostly Weaker
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia ended in negative territory today as key indices retreated amid cautious sentiment.

The benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBMKLCI) fell 11.31 points, or 0.65%, to close at 1,739.54. Broader indices also weakened, with the FBM70 down 21.95 points (-0.12%) to 17,569.67 and the FBM Emas Index sliding 67.24 points (-0.53%) to 12,738.20.

The FBM Shariah Index declined 39.63 points (-0.32%) to 12,222.01, while the F4GBM Index lost 5.56 points (-0.54%) to 1,026.22.

Active counters were mostly lower, led by Zentech and Zetrix in traded volume. Zentech topped the list with 928,379 shares traded, falling 0.5 sen to 0.5 sen.

Zetrix slipped 2 sen to 80 sen with 876,283 shares done, while Hock Soon dropped 4 sen to 56 sen on volume of 641,861 shares.

Tanco bucked the trend, rising 3 sen to RM1.41 with 533,934 shares traded. PharmaNiaga eased 1.5 sen to 30.5 sen on turnover of 416,273 shares.

data.title: Roller Coaster Week For Bitcoin, Are The Bearish Signals Over?
data.description:
The cryptocurrency market saw heightened volatility during the week of Feb 9-13, with Bitcoin and Ethereum retreating amid profit-taking and persistent macro uncertainty.

Bitcoin started the week near US$69,800 but struggled to sustain momentum, briefly testing key support levels around US$68,000 before sliding toward the mid-US$66,000 range by Feb 13. Despite intermittent rebounds, the cryptocurrency faced selling pressure tied to broader risk-off sentiment and ahead of key US economic data releases.

Ethereum, meanwhile, mirrored Bitcoin’s trajectory, trading around US$2,100 early in the week before weakening and dipping below the US$2,000 mark at one stage, reflecting continued bearish technical signals and cautious investor sentiment.

Both cryptocurrencies ended the week largely lower or flat compared with earlier gains, underscoring fragile market confidence.

Institutional flows also highlighted caution, with Bitcoin and Ethereum spot exchange-traded funds recording significant net outflows during the week, contributing to downward pressure on prices. Analysts noted the market is in a consolidation phase after recent sharp declines, with prices stabilising but lacking strong bullish catalysts.

Overall, the week reflected a consolidation period for crypto markets, as investors weighed macroeconomic signals, institutional flows and technical levels, leaving Bitcoin and Ethereum trading near key psychological support zones.

data.title: Weekly Recap: Kospi Hits Record High Above 5,500 Before Profit-Taking Pullback
data.description:
South Korean equities ended the week of Feb 9-13 on a mixed note, with the benchmark Kospi hitting fresh record highs before retreating on late profit-taking ahead of the Chinese New Year holiday.

The Kospi rallied strongly in the first half of the week, driven by gains in semiconductor and blue-chip counters, and surged 3.13% on Feb 12 to close at a historic high of 5,522.27, marking its first-ever break above the 5,500 milestone.

Tech heavyweights led the advance, with semiconductor stocks benefitting from sustained optimism over artificial intelligence-driven demand and strong institutional and foreign buying.

However, the rally lost steam by Feb 13 as investors locked in profits ahead of the extended holiday closure. The Kospi slipped 0.28% to finish at 5,507.01, snapping a four-day winning streak despite briefly touching another intraday record above 5,580. Foreign investors turned net sellers late in the week, while retail and institutional investors provided partial support.

Despite the pullback, overall sentiment remained constructive. The index continued to trade near record levels, underpinned by strong momentum in technology shares and robust institutional inflows, highlighting resilience in South Korea’s equity market following its recent surge.

The week ultimately underscored the Kospi’s bullish trajectory, even as short-term consolidation emerged after its historic breakout.

data.title: STI Starts Week On Softer Footing
data.description:
Singapore shares opened lower on Monday, tracking regional weakness as investors traded cautiously in early deals.

The benchmark Straits Times Index (STI) slipped 15.91 points, or 0.32%, to 4,921.87 as at 9.05am. Market breadth was negative, with 98 decliners outpacing 88 advancers.

Trading activity was moderate in early session, with 107.11 million shares worth S$134.78 million changing hands.

Among the index constituents, DBS Group Holdings edged down 0.05% to S$57.01. OCBC Bank traded at S$21.10, while United Overseas Bank stood at S$38.30. Singtel changed hands at S$4.87 and Genting Singapore was at S$0.765. Transport operator ComfortDelGro traded at S$1.49.

Across broader indices, the iEdge Singapore Next 50 Index stood at 1,513.52, while the iEdge S-REIT Index was at 1,109.05.

In derivatives trading, volume reached 11,560 contracts in early trade. The MSCI Singapore Index Futures February contract was last at 453.25, while regional contracts such as Nikkei 225 Index Futures March 2026 traded at 56,950.00.

Currency futures saw USD/SGD February contracts at 1.2636, with INR/USD February futures recording volume of 173 contracts.

Market participants are expected to monitor regional cues and macroeconomic developments for further direction as the session progresses.

data.title: UTDPLT, HLIND Lead Top Gainers On Bursa As Nestle, MPI Head Losers
data.description:
Several heavyweight counters led Bursa Malaysia’s top gainers list today, with UTD Plantation and Hong Leong Industries posting the strongest advances.

UTD Plantation rose 56 sen to RM30.18 to top the gainers board, while Hong Leong Industries climbed 36 sen to RM18.98. Quality Concrete added 25 sen to RM1.28, Heineken Malaysia gained 20 sen to RM24.60, and Batu Kawan edged up 14 sen to RM19.74.

On the losing side, consumer and financial heavyweights dominated decliners, led by Nestle and Malaysian Pacific Industries (MPI).

Nestle fell RM1.60 to RM109.00, while MPI slid RM1.22 to RM29.78. Hong Leong Bank dropped 58 sen to RM24.12 and Hong Leong Financial Group shed 58 sen to RM22.10, while Allianz declined 48 sen to RM22.50.

data.title: Market Snapshot: Hang Seng Index Retreats On Tech Sell-Off, Profit-Taking
data.description:
Trading on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange closed out a choppy week with broad weakness as sentiment swung from early optimism to profit-taking ahead of the Chinese New Year holiday.

The Hang Seng Index lost ground overall, slipping for much of the week before ending lower on Feb 13, with the index down about 1.7% at 26,567.12, pressured by selling in technology and China-linked stocks.

The week kicked off with upside momentum on Feb 9, as artificial intelligence and healthcare-related names attracted buying interest and helped lift broader sentiment. But as the sessions progressed, heavyweight tech shares, including major internet and cloud names, weakened, prompting investors to trim positions and driving a rotation into more defensive or cyclical segments.

Despite the overall pullback, market breadth wasn’t uniformly negative as a number of smaller stocks reached new 52-week highs by Feb 13, underscoring pockets of resilience amid the weakness. Two new listings also added fresh liquidity to the market during the week, although broader risk appetite remained subdued.

Trading volumes thinned as the holiday approached, with participants positioned cautiously ahead of the extended closure starting next week. Analysts see the near-term outlook as balanced, with investors watching for macro direction from mainland China and global markets once normal trading resumes.

data.title: Hong Kong Stocks Slide As Tech Shares Weigh On Market
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks closed lower on Feb 13, dragged down by weakness in technology and mainland Chinese shares as investors turned cautious after recent gains.

The Hang Seng Index fell 465.42 points, or 1.72%, to end at 26,567.12, with selling pressure concentrated in heavyweight tech and China-linked counters. The decline reflected renewed concerns over the technology sector outlook and the pace of China’s economic recovery.

Market sentiment remained fragile as investors trimmed exposure to growth-sensitive sectors, particularly technology, amid global uncertainty and valuation concerns following the market’s earlier rally.

Analysts said the pullback underscores lingering caution in Hong Kong equities, with investors awaiting clearer economic signals and fresh catalysts to drive the next phase of market direction.

data.title: Singapore Stocks Open Lower As STI Retreats From Recent Highs
data.description:
Singapore equities opened lower on Feb 13, with the Straits Times Index (STI) easing 0.81% to 4,976.21, retreating from its recent record above the 5,000‑point mark.

Market sentiment was weighed by profit-taking in heavyweight banking and blue-chip counters, following the benchmark’s historic milestone earlier this week.

Early trading also saw selective weakness across consumer and property-related stocks, offset slightly by gains in tech and mid-cap counters.

Investors are keeping a close eye on market direction after the STI’s recent rally, with cautious positioning ahead of upcoming corporate earnings and regional cues.

data.title: Singapore Exchange STI Slips Below 5,000 On Profit-Taking
data.description:
Singapore stocks retreated on Feb 13 as investors locked in gains following the benchmark Straits Times Index’s (STI) historic break above the 5,000 mark a day earlier.

The STI fell 78.98 points, or 1.57%, to close at 4,937.78, reversing part of its recent rally after hitting a record high. The pullback was led by declines in heavyweight banking stocks, while other blue chips also traded lower amid broader market consolidation.

Market participants said the move reflected profit-taking after the index’s strong run-up, with sentiment turning cautious despite supportive domestic fundamentals.

Analysts expect near-term volatility as investors rebalance positions, though Singapore equities remain among the region’s stronger performers year to date.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Extends Losses At Midday As Decliners Outpace Gainers
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia remained in negative territory at midday, weighed down by broad-based selling as losers sharply outnumbered gainers, reflecting cautious investor sentiment.

At 12:30pm, the benchmark FBM KLCI fell 8.43 points, or 0.48%, to 1,742.42 after trading between 1,741.57 and 1,751.38.

Broader indices also retreated. The FBM ACE Market Index slid 24.36 points to 4,750.77, reflecting weakness in small-cap and growth stocks, while the FBM Emas Index declined 52.14 points, or 0.41%, to 12,753.30. Other key indices mirrored the softer tone. The FBM 70 Index slipped 25.28 points to 17,566.34, while the FBM Shariah Index eased 26.18 points to 12,235.46.

Market breadth was firmly negative, with 639 decliners versus 300 gainers, while 494 counters were unchanged.

Trading activity, however, remained robust, with turnover reaching 1.29 billion shares valued at RM1.13 billion, indicating continued participation despite the cautious mood.

The midday decline suggests investors are locking in recent gains and adopting a wait-and-see stance, with sentiment likely to hinge on fresh catalysts and regional market direction heading into the afternoon session.

data.title: Profit-Taking Persists On Hang Seng Index, RHB
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) has maintained a long trading bias on Hang Seng Index Futures (HSIF), although the contract remains under pressure amid continued profit-taking and weakening momentum.

The research house noted that HSIF extended its losing streak last Friday, falling 456 points to close at 26,530 points after reversing from an intraday high of 27,120 points. The contract later edged up 30 points in the evening session to 26,560 points.

According to RHB Research, the latest price action, together with a declining relative strength index and trading below the 20-day simple moving average, indicates that sellers currently hold the technical advantage. Should the correction persist, the index may drift towards the 25,400-point support level.

However, a rebound could see HSIF move towards the 28,600-point resistance, followed by 29,100 points. The research house said the positive trading bias remains intact as long as the contract holds above the 26,400-point threshold, which also serves as the revised stop-loss level to manage downside risk.

Immediate support is seen at 26,400 points, with the next support at 25,500 points, while resistance levels are pegged at 28,600 points and 29,100 points.

data.title: Weekly Review: STI Hits Record High But Ends Week On Softer Note
data.description:
The benchmark Singapore Exchange closed the week of Feb 9-13 on a cautious footing after a volatile stretch that saw the Straits Times Index (STI) scale a fresh record before retreating on late profit-taking.

The STI rallied strongly in the first half of the week, briefly breaching the psychologically significant 5,000-point mark for the first time, lifted by gains in banking and blue-chip counters. Optimism over regional growth prospects and resilient corporate earnings underpinned buying momentum.

However, sentiment turned more measured heading into Feb 13 as investors locked in gains following the sharp run-up. Banking heavyweights led the pullback, dragging the index off its peak, while broader market breadth turned mixed.

Despite the late-week dip, overall trading activity remained firm, reflecting sustained institutional participation. Analysts expect near-term consolidation as investors digest recent gains and await fresh economic cues.

data.title: Wall Street Ends Mixed As Cooling Inflation Offsets AI Jitters
data.description:
US stocks ended mixed on Feb 13, with the S&P 500 barely higher after softer inflation data bolstered rate-cut hopes, while the Nasdaq declined as heavyweight technology and communications stocks weakened amid persistent concerns over artificial intelligence disruption.

Reuters reported that the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 48.95 points, or 0.1%, to 49,500.93. The S&P 500 edged up 3.41 points, or 0.05%, to 6,836.17, while the Nasdaq Composite fell 50.48 points, or 0.22%, to 22,546.67.

For the week, all three major indexes posted their steepest declines since November, with the S&P 500 down 1.39%, the Nasdaq sliding 2.1% and the Dow losing 1.23%, reflecting heightened volatility in technology stocks tied to AI competition and elevated capital spending.

Investor sentiment was initially lifted after inflation data showed US consumer prices rose less than expected in January, prompting traders to raise the probability of a June rate cut to 52.3%, according to CME Group’s FedWatch tool. However, technology giants including Nvidia and Apple dragged on the broader market.

Defensive sectors led gains, with utilities climbing 2.69% and real estate rising 1.48%. Healthcare stocks also advanced, as Dexcom jumped 7.6% and Moderna gained 5.3% following strong earnings.

Chip equipment maker Applied Materials surged 8.1% after issuing upbeat revenue and profit forecasts, while networking firm Arista Networks rose 4.8% on strong annual revenue guidance.

In commodities-related stocks, steelmakers including Nucor and Steel Dynamics declined after Peter Navarro of the White House dismissed reports of tariff reductions.

Market breadth was positive, with advancing stocks outnumbering decliners on both the NYSE and Nasdaq, while trading volume totalled 18.61 billion shares, below the 20-day average.

data.title: Bursa Ends Streak On Lower Note As Investors Rein In On Profits
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia ended the trading week on a softer note after a mixed set of sessions as investors took profits ahead of the upcoming Chinese New Year break and reacted to global cues.

The benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) started the week with modest strength but trended lower as the week progressed. Early in the week, broader indices showed incremental gains, with the FBM Emas Index rising on Feb 9 before sentiment turned cautious in mid-week. By Feb 13, the FBM KLCI retreated 0.65% to close around 1,739.5, reflecting broad-based selling as losers outnumbered gainers across the market.

Trading activity remained subdued ahead of the holiday, with lighter turnover and a selective approach from investors. Profit-taking was evident, particularly in blue-chip names, as market participants locked in recent gains and positioned defensively.

On the ACE Market, small-cap and growth stocks exhibited mixed performance, with volume remaining uneven and overall indexes showing only marginal improvements over the week.

A notable event during the week was the strong debut of a newly listed counter on the ACE Market, where shares more than doubled on their first day of trading, underscoring pockets of speculative interest despite the broader cautious tone.

Market Outlook: Analysts expect the local bourse to remain in a consolidative mode in the short term as traders return from the holiday period and await fresh macroeconomic catalysts.

data.title: Stock Picks: Northeast Group And Autocount Dotcom
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) has identified Northeast Group Bhd and Autocount Dotcom Bhd as stocks with potential technical upside, supported by improving price momentum and bullish chart patterns.

RHB Research said Northeast Group’s uptrend may resume if the stock forms an interim base near its recent breakout point.

The counter is trading above the RM0.62 resistance level, indicating that buyers currently hold the technical advantage. Following a period of consolidation, the share price could advance towards RM0.69 and subsequently RM0.76.

However, a decline below the RM0.55 support level would invalidate the positive outlook and may trigger a correction phase.

Autocount Dotcom is also seen extending its gains after staging a bullish breakout on strong trading volume.

The stock formed a long bullish candlestick and closed above the RM0.70 resistance level, signalling strong upward momentum.

Post-breakout, the counter may test RM0.75, with a further potential move towards RM0.80. A fall below RM0.65 would indicate the start of a corrective trend, according to the research house.
